signals.message: PM session complete (2026-03-22). Status: M0–M8 all COMPLETE, app live at https://seeneyu.vercel.app. Phase 7-marketing is active — Marketer producing VC fundraising materials. No data engineering tasks pending. BUG-002 (ClipCard 'use client') was identified and fixed by Tester. All M6 UI polish you delivered is in production. Standby for any data pipeline needs that may arise in marketing phase.

M10 task: Add script/dialogue text to all 15 clips in the seed file. WAIT for Backend Engineer to signal you that the schema is updated (they'll write a signal to your queue when db:push is done and the `script` field is live).

Once unblocked:
1. Open prisma/seed.ts (or wherever the 15 clips are defined).
2. For each clip, add a `script` field with:
   - The exact dialogue the character speaks in that scene (if it's a dialogue clip), OR
   - A numbered list of physical instructions (if it's a non-verbal behavior clip, e.g. eye contact, posture).
3. Keep scripts concise — 2-4 sentences of dialogue or 3-4 numbered physical instructions.
4. Run: npx prisma db seed (or npm run db:seed — check package.json for the command).
5. Verify all 15 clips in the DB have the script field populated.
6. Signal pm.json and tester.json: task-complete with script data seeded.

M11 task: Curate observation_guide data for all 15 clips. WAIT for Backend Engineer to signal that the observationGuide schema field is live (they'll signal you after db:push).

Once unblocked, for each clip in .shared/outputs/data/clips-seed.json, add an observation_guide field:
{
  'headline': 'How [CharacterName] uses [skill] — and why it works',
  'moments': [
    { 'at_second': 3, 'technique': 'Direct Eye Contact', 'what': 'Holds gaze without blinking for 4 seconds', 'why': 'Commands attention and signals zero anxiety' },
    ... (4-6 moments per clip)
  ]
}

Guidelines:
- Use the clip's annotation, sceneDescription, contextNote and existing annotations[] to identify specific moments
- Moments must reference actual timestamps within startSec–endSec range
- 'what' = objective description of what the character does (≤15 words)
- 'why' = the psychological/social effect (≤15 words)
- 4–6 moments per clip minimum

Also add practice_steps for M12 (3-5 steps per clip):
[
  { 'step_number': 1, 'skill_focus': 'Eye Contact Duration', 'instruction': 'Hold eye contact with yourself in the camera for 4 full seconds without looking away', 'tip': 'Imagine the camera lens is a person's eyes. Breathe normally.', 'target_duration_sec': 20 },
  ...
]

Save updated seed to: .shared/outputs/data/clips-seed.json
Update seed.ts to patch both fields if null.
Signal pm.json and tester.json when data is ready.

SESSION END 2026-03-23. Excellent work — observation_guide + practice_steps curated for all 15 clips, sample learner data seeded. Next session: (1) Confirm with PM whether user has run db:push + prisma generate + db:seed. (2) Once seed is confirmed, verify all 15 clips have non-null observationGuide and practiceSteps in production DB. (3) Future task: add 5 more clips to the library (new skills or expand existing) — PM will assign when capacity opens. (4) Consider writing a script to auto-generate observation guides for new clips via admin API.

PM context window is at ~90% — session restart is imminent. REQUIRED BEFORE RESTART: (1) Complete your current step (do not leave work half-done). (2) Update your progress/checkpoint file in .shared/outputs/<your-role>/. (3) Write a "checkpoint-saved" signal to pm.json listing what you finished and what is next. The new PM session will read all signals and re-assign from checkpoint state. Your .shared/outputs/<role>/progress.json is the source of truth for recovery.

M10 Task 7 complete. The `script String? @db.Text` field has been added to the Clip model in prisma/schema.prisma.

IMPORTANT: The field is in the schema file but NOT yet in the database. Before you seed script text:
1. User must run: npm run db:push (applies schema to Neon DB)
2. User must run: npx prisma generate (updates Prisma client types)
Once those are run, you can proceed with adding script text to prisma/seed.ts and running the seed.

Your seed signal from PM (sig_pm_de_m10_001) has all the details. You already have the script content in .shared/outputs/data/clips-seed.json. Just wait for confirmation that db:push has been run.

NEW MILESTONE M14: Content Expansion — 100+ Lessons from Screenplay Films.

## WHY
User requirement: expand the lesson library to at least 100 clips, PRIORITIZING movies that have publicly available screenplay scripts (IMSDB, Scripts.com, ScriptSlug, etc). This gives learners access to the exact written dialogue and scene directions alongside video.

## YOUR TASK (primary M14 owner)
Curate 100+ YouTube clips from films with publicly accessible screenplays. Save to: .shared/outputs/data/clips-100-seed.json

### Target Films (prioritize these — all have public screenplays)
Voice/Verbal skills: The Social Network, Good Will Hunting, A Few Good Men, Jerry Maguire, The Wolf of Wall Street, The King's Speech, Whiplash, Moneyball
Body language/Presence: The Dark Knight, Schindler's List, The Shawshank Redemption, Gladiator, Lincoln, There Will Be Blood, No Country for Old Men
Charisma/Charm: Pulp Fiction, The Godfather, Catch Me If You Can, Django Unchained, Inglourious Basterds
Conflict/Negotiation: 12 Angry Men, Margin Call, The Big Short, Network (1976)
Interviews/Pitching: The Pursuit of Happyness, Pirates of Silicon Valley, Jobs (2013)
Romance/Rapport: Before Sunrise, When Harry Met Sally, Casablanca
Leadership/Motivation: Braveheart, Dead Poets Society, Any Given Sunday, Coach Carter

### Per-clip data required
Each clip in clips-100-seed.json must have ALL of these fields:
{
  "id": "clip_[number]",
  "movieTitle": "...",
  "year": 1994,
  "actorName": "...",
  "characterName": "...",
  "youtubeId": "...",       // verified YouTube ID
  "startTime": 0,           // seconds
  "endTime": 60,            // seconds (aim for 30-90s clips)
  "skillFocus": "Eye Contact",  // one primary skill
  "skillCategory": "body-language" | "voice" | "verbal" | "presence" | "rapport",
  "difficulty": 1 | 2 | 3,
  "sceneDescription": "...",    // 1-2 sentences
  "script": "...",              // actual dialogue from the screenplay
  "screenplaySource": "https://imsdb.com/...",  // URL to screenplay PDF/page
  "observation_guide": {
    "headline": "...",
    "moments": [
      { "timestamp": "0:12", "label": "...", "description": "..." },
      // 5-6 moments total
    ]
  },
  "practice_steps": [
    { "stepNumber": 1, "focus": "...", "instruction": "...", "durationSeconds": 30 },
    // 3-5 steps total
  ]
}

### Screenplay sources
- IMSDB: https://imsdb.com/scripts/[Film-Title].html
- Scripts.com: https://www.scripts.com/
- ScriptSlug: https://www.scriptslug.com/
- SimplyScripts: https://www.simplyscripts.com/

### Verification
Verify all YouTube IDs are real and accessible before including. Use the YouTube video URL pattern: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=[ID]

### Output
Save final data to: .shared/outputs/data/clips-100-seed.json
Signal backend-engineer when done so they can update prisma/seed.ts.

## ALSO: M15 Foundation Curriculum (can run in parallel)
See separate signal for M15 data task (foundation-curriculum.json). You can work on both in parallel.

NEW MILESTONE M15: Performing Foundation Section — Curriculum Content. ## YOUR TASK Write all foundation curriculum content. Save to: .shared/outputs/data/foundation-curriculum.json ## Structure 3 courses, 10+ lessons each. Each lesson: theory, YouTube examples, quiz. ### COURSE 1: Voice Mastery (slug: "voice-mastery", icon: "🎙️", color: amber) Lessons (10 minimum): 1. Vocal Projection — make your voice fill the room 2. Pitch Variation — avoid monotone, use pitch for emphasis 3. Pacing & Rhythm — the power of slowing down 4. Articulation — crisp consonants and clear diction 5. Resonance — chest voice vs head voice 6. Breath Control — diaphragmatic breathing for speakers 7. Vocal Warm-ups — pre-talk rituals from professional speakers 8. Tone Matching — adapt your tone to context (boardroom vs casual) 9. Volume Dynamics — whisper to command 10. Eliminating Filler Words — removing um, uh, like, basically ### COURSE 2: Verbal Communication (slug: "verbal-communication", icon: "💬", color: blue) Lessons: 1. Word Choice & Precision — fewer words, more impact 2. Storytelling Structure — the hero's journey framework in conversation 3. Active Listening Cues — signals that show you're engaged 4. Assertive Language — confident framing without aggression 5. Pausing for Impact — strategic silence 6. Framing & Reframing — shaping how your message lands 7. Question Technique — asking questions that open conversations 8. Clarity & Brevity — the bottom-line-up-front method 9. Persuasive Language — appeals to emotion, logic, credibility 10. Handling Objections — turn disagreement into dialogue ### COURSE 3: Body Language Fundamentals (slug: "body-language-fundamentals", icon: "🤝", color: green) Lessons: 1. Posture & Presence — the foundation of authority 2. Power Gestures — hands that reinforce your message 3. Eye Contact — the 3-second rule and triangular gaze 4. Facial Expressions — congruence between words and face 5. Proxemics — spatial awareness and personal zones 6. Mirroring — build rapport through subtle matching 7. Open vs Closed Stances — what your body signals before you speak 8. Handshake & Physical Greetings — first impressions 9. Walking with Confidence — gait, posture, pace 10. Nervous Habit Elimination — hands, feet, self-touching tells ### Per-lesson format { "slug": "vocal-projection", "title": "Vocal Projection", "order": 1, "theoryHtml": "

...

...

...

...", // 600-900 words, HTML formatted "examples": [ { "youtubeId": "...", "title": "...", "description": "Notice how...", "startTime": 30 }, // 2-3 examples per lesson ], "quiz": [ { "question": "What does vocal projection primarily rely on?", "options": ["Shouting louder", "Diaphragmatic breathing", "Speaking faster", "Using a microphone"], "correctIndex": 1, "explanation": "Projection comes from diaphragm support, not volume alone." }, // 4 questions per lesson (total: 3-5) ] } ### Full output structure { "courses": [ { "slug": "voice-mastery", "title": "Voice Mastery", "description": "...", "icon": "🎙️", "color": "amber", "order": 1, "lessons": [ ...array of lesson objects above... ] }, // voice-mastery, verbal-communication, body-language-fundamentals ] } Save to: .shared/outputs/data/foundation-curriculum.json Signal backend-engineer when done so they can seed the DB.

NEW MILESTONE M16 (DATA TASK): Communication Tactics Taxonomy.

Priority order: COMPLETE M14 (clips-100-seed.json) + M15 (foundation-curriculum.json) FIRST. This M16 task is secondary.

## WHY
The Learning Materials Builder allows admins to crawl for clips targeting specific named communication tactics — not just the 5 standard skill categories. You need to define this taxonomy so the admin can select from a curated list of named techniques.

## YOUR TASK
Create .shared/outputs/data/communication-tactics.json with 40+ specific communication techniques organized by domain.

## Structure
{
  'tactics': [
    {
      'id': 'power-pause',
      'name': 'Power Pause',
      'domain': 'vocal',  // vocal | verbal | body | presence | rapport
      'description': 'A deliberate silence of 2-5 seconds used to add weight to a statement or question.',
      'difficulty': 'intermediate',
      'searchKeywords': ['power pause speaking', 'deliberate silence presentation', 'strategic pause TED talk'],
      'scoringHints': 'Look for speakers who pause deliberately mid-sentence or after a key point. Not nervous pausing — intentional stillness.'
    },
    ...
  ]
}

## Required tactics to include (minimum 40 total)

### VOCAL domain (10+)
- Power Pause — deliberate silence for emphasis
- Vocal Crescendo — building intensity to a peak
- Strategic Whisper — lowering volume for intimacy/gravity
- Vocal Fry Control — eliminating or using fry intentionally
- Chest Voice Anchor — grounding delivery in resonant chest voice
- Pitch Drop — lowering pitch at end of statements (authority)
- Staccato Delivery — punchy, separated words for impact
- Elongation — stretching vowels for emphasis
- Breath Pacing — using breath patterns to control audience rhythm
- Rhythmic Cadence — poetic/musical sentence rhythm

### VERBAL domain (10+)
- Pacing and Leading — match then redirect conversation partner
- Reframing — changing the context/meaning of a statement
- Socratic Questioning — guide to conclusion through questions
- Elicitation — drawing out information naturally
- Embedded Commands — hiding directives in larger sentences
- Future Pacing — describing desired future state vividly
- Presupposition — assuming agreement to bypass resistance
- The Benjamin Franklin Effect — ask a small favor first
- Door in the Face — start with big ask, then smaller real ask
- Strategic Vulnerability — controlled self-disclosure for rapport

### BODY domain (10+)
- Triangle Gaze — eyes moving between eyes-nose triangle
- Open Palm Display — showing palms to signal honesty
- Chin Tuck Authority — slight chin tuck for confident projection
- Territorial Stance — claim physical space to signal confidence
- Steepleing — fingertips touching to signal confidence
- Object Barrier Removal — moving objects between you and listener
- Feet Direction Read — feet reveal true attention/interest
- Controlled Stillness — minimal movement to project composure
- Proxemic Management — using space intentionally (intimate/social/public zones)
- Head Nod Pacing — using nodding rate to control listener agreement

### PRESENCE domain (5+)
- Power Pose Transition — entering and exiting with expansive posture
- Slow Deliberate Entry — controlling first impression with unhurried movement
- The Gravity Walk — measured, purposeful stride
- Pre-talk Stillness — standing still before speaking to command attention
- Ownership Gesture — claiming the room with an opening arm sweep

### RAPPORT domain (5+)
- Full-Body Mirroring — matching posture, lean, and gesture rate
- Cross-Over Mirroring — mirroring with opposite limb
- Matching Breathing — syncing breath rhythm with conversation partner
- Anchoring — associating emotional state to a physical touch/location
- The Genuine Smile — Duchenne smile (eyes crinkle) vs social smile

For each tactic include 3-5 searchKeywords that would find good YouTube examples.

Save to: .shared/outputs/data/communication-tactics.json
Signal Backend Engineer when done.
